Art title 1: Too Pleasing for Our Sinful Eyes
Artist Statement 1:
This was done from a dark place, feeling very much in touch with the dark side of me. This was my honest expression, using my artistic abilities to express my angst. 

This was just a glimpse of one moment, but I think we should be able to do this, and still at the end of the day say, God you are sovereign.
Art title 2:
ART:
The Best Relationships Have Ups and Downs
Artist Statement 2: Even the best relationshiops have ups and downs. Perhaps those that have ups and downs have potential to experience deeper patience, love and forgiveness. So when you're not feeling it with God, or a person, remember it doesn't necessarily mean it's not meant to be.
Story Title again: Best Relationships Have Ups and Downs
excerpt:
The night before, he lay in the bed next to his wife, fighting his thoughts, "Everything she says bothers me. It's been like this for too long. I've just lost the love for her." He battled this audio track in his head for hours.

But now he waited for her in the restaurant. She was coming from work. His heart had been knotted all day. He decided to do his Bible reading to center himself as he waited for her. He was looking for any last message from God that might influence what he felt he finally had to do.

He had been reading through the prophets. The reading for today was Zechariah 14. He read it and set down his phone. He put his head in his hands and prayed, "I just really haven't liked what you've said for a long time either. I mean, sinners having their eyes rot in their sockets while they stand there?" He shook his head and threw up his hands. "It's been a long time since I've felt love for you too!"

Just then the host led his wife to his table. She took the chair across from him and moved it to the edge of the table close to him. As she sat down she looked at him with longing in her eyes. "This place is so cute, Babe." She grasped his hand and said, "I know last night was hard baby. I missed you, today."

He nodded as tears filled his eyes. He looked away, down by his feet for a moment. Then he picked up something. He handed her a large bouquet of flowers and a card. She read it and her eyes filled with love anew for him. She took his face into her hands, put her forehead to his and said, "I'm so sorry too, babe. I'm entirely yours still too, even through the hard seasons."

And like that they were holding each other and all the feelings were back. As the waiter set down the dinner, she leaned into him and whispered, "I think the ups and downs make this love even more real."

A moment later he prayed for their meal and his prayer included, "God, thank you that the best relationships have ups and downs. It makes our love, even more real."

---
Raw Spoon, July 2, 2022
Blog excerpt:
I've been doing a lot of internal work lately. Some problems with my closest relationships have suggested I have some seriously deep stuff to work on. During this time several separate friends suggested different books/ideologies which basically say we should revisit old memories to see if something is dictating our current behaviors. 


And through it I've discovered this really interesting method that, although I'm not sure my problems have all gone away, has revealed MANY interesting things I wasn't aware of about myself and my life! 


Maybe like you, this quarantining has been super hard for me, especially in the last month or so. But I realized that maybe we can use this forced solidarity for something good, and looking deeper into ourselves is a good candidate for that.


Before I tell you about this method, I'll tell you about the three books/ideologies which convinced me revisiting our memories has value. 

Attachment theory, introduced by John Bowlby, basically says about 40% of the population either push romantic partners away (avoidant attachment), or need them too much (anxious attachment). And the theory claims that the cause of it happened between you and your primary care giver in the first years of life. 

"Emotionally Healthy Spirituality," by Peter Scazzero, says we can't be truly spiritually healthy until we address the psychological needs that we try to get met by manipulating others. 

Arthur Janov, who wrote "Primal Scream," says his method of therapy brings people back to traumatic memories and encourages them to fully experience the emotions they had suppressed from that young age. He said after they release their 'primal scream' his patients' neuroses go away.

Also, I've been listening to Malcolm Gladwell's "Revisionist History" podcast and Season 3, episode 4 talks about how malleable memories really are! So it just lends credibility to the usefulness of revisiting, and reframing these memories.


So, all this was enough to convince me I should explore it. Here's what I've been doing, and almost every time I do it, it reveals new things I hadn't realized before about my childhood and current behavior.
